entry.title: Re: Recalculate the components quantity automatically whe...
entry.content:
What you are seeing is actually standard system behavior. When you change the finished goods quantity during GR in MIGO, the system does not automatically recalculate the component quantities. The reason is that the component consumption is determined by the subcontracting BOM and the quantities assigned in the purchase order. MIGO simply posts against those values — it doesn’t re‑explode the BOM dynamically.
If proportional consumption is always required, implement a BAdI enhancement so that when FG quantity changes, the system recalculates component quantities based on the BOM ratio. If subcontractor consumption varies, keep manual adjustment, since automatic recalculation may not reflect reality.

entry.title: Kyma Evolution: Transforming SAP Kyma into a tailor-made SaaS Platform for sbs extensions
entry.content:
Introduction

Hello SAP Community!

As an Extensibility Expert, I’m constantly looking for the most efficient ways to build and operate enterprise-grade extensions for the SAP Cloud ERP following the Clean Core principles. We all know the Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) is the go-to framework for Cloud Native side-by-side Extensions on BTP, but where you run them on large scale matters just as much as how you code it. 

Today, I’m starting a 3-part series (Part 2, Part 3 ) "Kyma Evolution". We will explore how the powercouple of Kyma in combination with the CAP Operator provides a scalable, high-performance, cost-effective runtime for CAP Multitenancy SaaS applications. In case you don’t know SAP Kyma and its benefits yet, please have a look at the Kyma Learning which explains it quite well.  

The paradigm shift: From monolithic to a modular environment

For a long time, SAP Kyma was seen as a fixed bundle of tools. You got everything, whether you needed it or not. That has changed. With the Kyma Module Concept, the platform is now modular and extensible.  Even more exciting is the introduction of Community Modules. This means Kyma is no longer limited to what SAP provides out-of-the-box. The community can now contribute extensions. One of the most powerful examples so far is the CAP Operator.  By adding the CAP Operator as a module, you transform a generic Kyma cluster into a specialized CAP SaaS Runtime.

The "Smart Broker": Why the CAP Operator is a game-changer

When we compare Kyma to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry, the advantages of using a dedicated Operator become clear. While Cloud Foundry is a great general-purpose platform, the CAP Operator on Kyma acts as a "smart broker" specifically for CAP Applications.

Key Benefits for Partners and Customers:

  • 100% Automated Lifecycle Management: The Operator understands CAP. It handles application deployment, tenant management, DB model updates, and service bindings automatically and new capabilities like SAP HANA Cloud Native tenants (Videocast) including tenant backup and restore management can be added once available.
  • Cost-Efficiency via Higher Container Density: In contrast to Cloud Foundry, Kyma allows for fine-grained resource limits. This means you can run more services on the same infrastructure, lowering your BTP bill when you are running multiple CAP Applications.
  • Cost-Efficiency via Resource Sharing: Expensive Services like SAP HANA Cloud and Cloud Logging can be shared in CF and Kyma. However we will see in Part 2 that in the Kyma environment this will be more elegant and simplified using the CAP Operator.
  • Enterprise Resilience & Day-2 Ops: It provides built-in "Reconciliation Loops." If a tenant's database connection fails, the Operator detects and fixes it without human intervention.
  • Advanced Networking & Security (Zero Trust Architecture): One of the most significant advantages over Cloud Foundry is how Kyma handles connectivity. In Cloud Foundry, preventing a service from being publicly accessible often requires manual effort. In Kyma, thanks to the CAP Operator's integration with Istio, your internal  services stay cluster-internal by default. You have full control over the attack surface.
  • Integrated Domain Management: Managing Custom Domains becomes a native experience using the CAP Operator. Instead of juggling external services for certificates, you can orchestrate your SaaS-brand URLs directly within the Kyma environment, providing a seamless, secure and automated experience.

The ecosystem enablers: Extensibility using CAP Plugins + Kyma Modules

SAP is providing enablers for the ecosystem on different levels:

  1. At the Infrastructure Level (Kyma): We use Modules (like the CAP Operator) to make the runtime "CAP-aware."
  2. At the Application Level (CAP): We use CAP Plugins to add business features like multitenancy or audit logging with a single command.
  3. At CAP CLI Level: We can add plugins like CAP Operator Plugin to the cds cli which provides capabilities to generate CAP Operator resources essential for deploying multi-tenant CAP Applications from your project setup.

This synergy creates the ultimate development and operations experience. You use plugins to build fast and the Operator to run smart and it enables you to contribute and to add your specific capabilities at any level.

Turning Kyma into a CAP Runtime

The beauty of this evolution is simplicity. You can now add the CAP Operator Community Module directly from your Kyma Cockpit using the Add Modules or Modify Modules Button on the Cluster Details Screen.

The Modules overview shows you the SAP Provided and Managed Modules under the Module Pane and the installed Community Modules in a separate Pane.

As a next step use the Add button on the Community Module Pane.

On the next screen you need to choose the Add Source YAML’s button to get the list of community modules. 

Just keep the defaults and press the Add button.

This will load the list of currently available Community Modules from which you need to check the cap-operator Tile and press Add. 

This will start the automatic installation of the CAP Operator Components into the namespace called cap-operator-system. 

The installation and module status will be displayed in the overview and will turn into green after installation is successfully finished.

By doing this, you aren't just deploying code; you are creating an intelligent system that manages your S/4HANA Multitenancy extensions built with CAP for you as it adds Custom Resources (CRs) like CAPApplication, CAPApplicationVersion, CAP Tenant and other to your cluster. This will enable: 

  • Quick and easy deployment of CAP application backends, router, and related networking components.
  • Integration with SAP Software-as-a-Service Provisioning service to handle asynchronous tenant subscription requests, executing provisioning / deprovisioning tasks as Kubernetes jobs.
  • Automated upgrades of known tenants as soon as new application versions are available.
  • Support for deployment of service-specific content / configuration as a Kubernetes job with every application version (for example, HTML5 application content to SAP HTML5 Application Repository Service).
  • Management of TLS certificates and DNS entries related to the deployed application, with support of customer-specific domains.

and further capabilities.

What’s Next?

In Part 2, we will dive deeper. We’ll look in more details of the runtime and at a cost benefits to prove why this modular approach is a win for your bottom line. What are your thoughts on the modular Kyma concept? Have you tried the CAP Operator yet? Have you ideas for additional community modules for the ecosystem?

entry.title: Re: Unable to Change House Bank in Open Item of Advance R...
entry.content:
Good day @Jessicaaguilar1 

Thank you for your questions.

Question: Should it be possible to change the house bank in the open item of an advance request? Why does the system prevent changing the house bank?

Answer: The simple answer is NO.  See KBA 2811991.  So, what you are experiencing is expected in public cloud for CME F down payment request. The reasons for that are:

  • Down payments are requests and references for a later payment that the system expects to be enriched with payment execution attributes.
  • The system determines the House Bank at payment execution, not maintained on the line item (as you tried to do). In Public Cloud, the House Bank is normally determined by the payment process. Therefore, it will not allow you to make ad hoc changes on the posted line item to avoid you bypassing the standard determination and controls.

So, your error message aligns with those requirements.

Instead of trying to change the house bank in the open item, you should:

  • Use the payment process to select or influence the bank, instead of changing the line item. You can maintain bank determination for paying company code and payment method in your CBC. See KBA
  • Alternatively, consider using Create Single Payment directly. Here you can edit the bank fields. See KBA 2709184 and KBA 3226345 for more insights.

Here is a quick guide on how to navigate that:

  • Open the Create Single Payment app,

  • enter company code, supplier , payment method. Choose your required House Bank and select relevant open item you want to pay and post.

To summarise:

  • The public cloud does not permit changing House Bank on a CME F noted item.
  • The House Bank belongs to payment execution and bank determination, not for manual edits on supplier line item.
  • Use bank determination configuration or Create Single Payment to control the paying bank.

Let me know if you have further questions. 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: How to reuse employee id after cancelling the ONB with rehire functionality enabled
entry.content:
Hi ONB Experts,

Need your help urgently. We have 20 candidate cases, wherein we are not able to complete the onboarding process on account of below error due to incorrect event reason flowing from RCM. 

While attempting to complete the Review New Hire Data step, the system is generating the following error:

"You cannot create a job information record without the recruit record for user XXX. The first job information record must be the recruit record."

Following the KBA solution (https://me.sap.com/notes/3356218/E), we tried changing the event reason (where the event is “Hire”), but then encountered this error:

"You're not allowed to change the event reason if the event is also changed from Rehire to Hire. Ensure that changing the event reason for this record does not change the event or employment status."

This results in a loop where we cannot change the event reason, but without changing it, we cannot proceed further.

Steps to reproduce the issue (including all user accounts involved):

  1. Login as Onboarding SPOC
  2. Navigate to the Onboarding Dashboard
  3. Search for the candidate
  4. Start the Review New Hire Data step
  5. Complete the required information
  6. Click Submit

At this point, the error below appears:

"jobInfo: You cannot create a job information record without the hire record for user [USERID]. The first job information record must be the hire record."

Root Cause Analysis:

    • The error is due to the event reason flowing incorrectly from RCM, i.e., "Replacement - Resignation (Rehire_Rep-Term-Resig)", which has the event as Rehire.
    • We attempted to change the event reason to "Replacement - Resignation (Hire_Rep_Term_Resig)", which is what was selected in RCM.
    • However, upon clicking Submit, the system returns:

"You're not allowed to change the event reason if the event is also changed from Rehire to Hire. Ensure that changing the event reason for this record does not change the event or employment status."

Thus, this blocks further progress.

Recent system changes prior to issue onset:

      • Created a custom picklist field cust_EventReason in the candidate application template.
      • Created a new picklist “EventReason” and mapped it to cust_EventReason.
      • Mapped the cust_EventReason in Recruit-to-Hire data mapping to ensure the event reason flows correctly from RCM to ONB, as per updated SAP 2H release guidelines.

Please advise on how to resolve this event reason flow conflict or workaround to proceed past this step without wasting employee id's.

Thank you

@Gopinath_Murugaiyan

entry.title: Re: Why Document Like it's 1999
entry.content: A good read! I think documentation is often overlooked, considered not needed when we are delivering requirements, but it really also can be a life saver. Good governance around documentation is also needed, the problem with even the best documentation, is the minute its written its out of date, with changes coming in all the time, and devs not always finding the time. One thing I have noticed whilst testing both Joule and copilot is that genAI is actually pretty decent at compiling documentation. Which is why I also think we need to look further to use AI tooling to help us generate good docs, potentially combining with discoverability and a replacement to RICEF where we focus on business components, with the structure above is a really good step in that direction. You could define the structure in a markup, and use a tool like backstage (opensource cataloging) to then semi automate your catalog build and documentation. If you can automate, the documentation can be auto updated anytime. Its a shame this kind of tooling is often overlooked by the vendors, but a good document really can help save a business.

entry.title: Re: How to configure managed approuter to serve CAP endpo...
entry.content:
In addition to the answer of Willem, you are using a standalone approuter so you have to open the app via the approuter and not the cap service. Authentication will be handled by the approuter and forward the requests to the cap service with a jwt token. To automatically redirect you to the right app in the approuter you can add a welcome page using the id of the app in the manifest.json , check this approuter xs-app.json example: https://github.com/lemaiwo/ui5con25-webc-demo/blob/main/approuter/xs-app.json

 

Also make sure you enable relative paths in the ui5 deploy config of the zipper task, see example here:

https://github.com/lemaiwo/ui5con25-tooling-demo/blob/1a373d691dd37fd7b4c87fdee17f222176822932/sample/ui5-deploy-cf.yaml#L23

Hope this helps!

entry.title: Re: "Schedule Billing Creation" Batch job collective billing
entry.content:
Good day @Thiru2203 

Thank you for your question.

The behaviour you are seeing is because collective billing is based on the standard billing split logic and it does not offer the same separate invoice per billing due list item possibility that you can use in Create Billing Documents. This is described in KBA 3510799.

Your VF04 settings does not change the background job result because:

  • The VF04 app provides a manual creation option like create separate billing documents for each item of the billing due list.
  • The Scheduling Billing creation job you are using does not provide that setting for automatic runs, so the job continues to combine items according to the standard split criteria. See KBA 3510799 for more details.

Question: Could you please advise if there is any configuration available to ensure that this job generates only one invoice per outbound delivery?

Answer: You cannot achieve this with pure configuration in public cloud. This is because delivery number is not a standard split criterion you can switch on in public cloud for this job. Which is contrary to what you can do in standard SD billing, where the system combines deliveries into one invoice when header data matches.

I recommende you:

  • Use manual billing with VF04. If your business can accept manual processing, I suggest you use Create Billing Documents and tick “Create separate billing documents for each item of the billing due list.”
  • Alternatively, you can force a split. You can use Custom Data Transfer for Billing Documents BAdI (SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER) for this purpose. This is also mentioned in KBA 3510799.

Quick summary:

  • In public cloud, Schedule Billing Creation will collectively bill based on standard split rules.
  • The separate per item option is not available for the scheduled job, so your setting there will not change the job behaviour.
  • You cannot achieve one invoice per delivery through standard configuration for this background job.
  • If you really need automation, consider forced split via a released extensibility.

Let me know if you have further questions.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 1, how to show search Recrods on SAP UI
entry.content:
We are using SAP Sales and Service Cloud, Version 1. We have developed a custom CTI widget (external web client application) that is:

  • Hosted on-premises
  • Embedded and running inside the SAP Activity Center UI
  • The CTI widget provides a search input where users can enter a name or phone number.
  • When a search is initiated:
    • The CTI widget calls our backend service.
    • The backend service uses SAP Sales and Service Cloud OData APIs to search for relevant business records (e.g., Contacts, Accounts).
    • The search results are returned and displayed in the CTI widget UI.

We want to open open and view the corresponding SAP business object directly in the SAP UI (for example, Contact or Account).

 Questions

  1.  Is it supported in SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 1 for a CTI widget hosted in the Activity Center to trigger navigation to SAP business objects (such as Account, Contact, etc.) in the SAP UI?
  2. If this is supported, what is the recommended and supported implementation approach?
  3. We attempted to trigger SAP UI navigation directly from the CTI widget (without using an HTML Mashup), but this approach did not work.
    The navigation mechanism we tried is described in the following SAP documentation: Mashup Callback Operations | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: How SAP CX AI Toolkit Integrations Create Real Business Value
entry.content:
The SAP CX AI Toolkit becomes most powerful when connected to the everyday systems your teams rely on: email, CRM, commerce data, and enterprise documents. These integrations allow the AI to give timely insights, automate prep work, and improve customer interaction

Below is a concise, business-friendly snapshot of what each integration delivers.

📧Microsoft 365

Turn emails, meetings, and files into instant insights
The toolkit can use Outlook, calendar events, and OneDrive files to help employees stay informed. 

Business value:

  • Sales gets a quick briefing before meetings: summaries of email threads, open items, and customer tone.
  • Support agents can quickly understand long email exchanges without reading everything.
  • Managers receive helpful daily digests highlighting risks or important follow-ups.

👥SAP Customer Data Platform (CDP)

See the customer as one unified profile
CDP data helps teams deliver more relevant and personalized customer experiences. 

Business value:

  • Marketing can generate targeted messages based on real customer behavior.
  • Sales reps instantly understand a prospect’s recent activity, interests, and engagement.
  • Customer success teams detect early churn signals.

🤝SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C)

Smarter sales and service actions using CRM data
C4C integration brings account, contact, and case details directly into AI workflows. 

Business value:

  • Sales gets one-click account summaries.
  • Service agents receive recommended next steps while viewing a case.

🛠SAP Sales & Service Cloud v2

Next-level CRM intelligence
This integration enriches AI suggestions with modern CRM data.

Business value:

  • Service teams get proactive recommendations based on past issues.
  • Sales leaders improve forecast accuracy with AI‑surfaced engagement patterns.

🛒SAP Commerce Cloud

Bring commerce activity into conversations
Commerce Cloud provides product and customer interaction data for AI-driven assistance. 

Business value:

  • Chatbots help shoppers with product questions and cross‑sell suggestions.
  • Merchandising sees which products customers ask about or struggle with.
  • Sales reps know what a customer browsed before calling.

📦Azure Blob Storage

Tap into your company’s document knowledge
Azure storage feeds additional documents into the AI‑powered Q&A engine.

Business value:

  • Field teams get instant answers from manuals or specs.
  • Product managers turn long documents into short summaries.

🔍Entity Matching

AI that recognizes your business IDs automatically
Rules allow AI to recognize references like “order 5531” or “case 1023” in messages. 

Business value:

  • Service agents jump straight to the right record without searching.
  • Sales reps get context instantly when documents or emails reference deal IDs.

✔ In Short

By connecting your most important business systems, the #SAP CX AI Toolkit becomes a practical, everyday assistant that

  • Prepares your teams faster
  • Reduces busywork
  • Improves customer interactions
  • Surfaces insights you’d otherwise miss

More info

Suggested learning.sap.com resources and what to look for:

  • Starting with AI in SAP Customer Experience Solutions 
  • Utilizing SAP CX AI Toolkit for SAP Commerce Cloud 
  • SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 - CX AI Toolkit, Gen AI & Machine Learning 
  • Building AI Tools and Agents for SAP CX Solutions 

Official product documentation

  • Setting Up your SAP CX AI Toolkit System

entry.title: SAP Business ByDesign 26.02 is now ready!
entry.content:
SAP Business ByDesign 26.02 is now ready!

SAP Business ByDesign 26.02 is now available and the SAP Business ByDesign What’s New blog gives you an overview about the most important new features of SAP Business ByDesign 26.02. These are the so called Focus Topics ( ).
For these Focus Topics, additional in-depth blogs and videos might be available - just click the link next to the Focus Topic in the What’s New blog.

To get an overview about all new features of SAP Business ByDesign in 26.02 please go to the What's New document.

Cross Topics

  • eInvoice Templates for SAP Business ByDesign
    Customers are now able to use XSLT-templates to send and receive eInvoices with SAP Business ByDesign (Blog).
  • E-Customer Invoice Output Scenario for Private Accounts
    User now can enable an “E-Invoicing Include PDF” option in output settings for customer invoices for private accounts . When selected, the system will attach a PDF file along with the XML output for e-invoicing.
  • Attachments can no longer be previewed in Browser
    Download only access ensures that sensitive documents are not opened inside the browser, reducing exposure to browser-based vulnerabilities.
  • Readable Data Source Name in Machine Learning Cockpit
    Users can now better understand and identify the data source avoiding the complexity of understanding technical IDs, making the MLC more intuitive.
  • Data Extraction Enhancements: Filters for Customer Creation Date and Status Filters for Supplier data
    By filtering based on creation date and status, users automatically pull only correct and up‑to‑date records.
  • Attachment Extraction – Self Service Feature: Customer Owned SharePoint
    Enable customers to extract all attachments from their Business ByDesign tenant to their own SharePoint, so they can manage attachment data independently without relying on SAP Operations.
  • New Built-In Support feature to report a Case to SAP Business ByDesign Support
    Key Users can now create and manage SAP support cases exclusively through the new Built‑In Support platform, replacing the older “Report a Case / Forward to Provider”.
  • New CompanyID field in creating workflow rules for Suppliers
    There is now a new CompanyID field when creating or modifying workflow rules for Suppliers which allows to set rules only for suppliers linked to a specific company.
For detailed information about What’s New in the Cross Topics area, please click here.

Customer Relationship 
Management

 

  • Enhanced Product description in Web Services
    Customer can now provide custom descriptions through web services for CoveredProducts and CoveredRegisteredProducts in customer quote and customer contract instead of changing it through UI.
  • Enhanced External Reference Column in Customer Contract Screen
    Customer Contract OWL screen is enhanced with a new column “External Reference”. 
  • Enhanced New project creation pop-up screen with required fields
    Users can now select the type of project (customer Projects) and date from which project has to be started within the project creation pop-up from sales order. 
  • Upgrade to the SAP Business ByDesign Add-in for Microsoft Outlook Integration 
    Upgraded version of Outlook add-in from the download center can be downloaded which will secure the application from being exposed to  vulnerabilities. 
For detailed information about What’s New in the Customer Relation. Management area, please click here.

Supplier Relationship 
Management

  • Add Purchase Order Approval Condition Net Value Change
    Customer are now able to choose an additional condition called Total Net Value Change in the purchase order header approval process.
  • Stop Purchase Order Release for Blocked/Obsolete Supplier in List Price
    With this Enhancement, purchase orders cannot be sent to incorrect or inappropriate suppliers.
  • Enhancements in the Supplier Invoice Report Header
    Customer are now able to find the payment terms in the supplier invoice analytics reports.
For detailed information about What’s New in the Supplier Relation. Management area, please click here.

Supply Chain 
Management

  • PSM read release in node ValidShippingConditions in 3 columns in
    BO TransportationLane
    PSM release for the fields Shipping Condition, Shipping Duration and Active Status in transport lane which can now be accessed by partners/customers.
  • Enhancement in  Physical Inventory Count report
    Users will now see the two new fields "Inventory Accuracy in % (Value)" and "Inventory Accuracy in % (Quantity)" in Physical Inventory Count report to provide better analytic reporting.
  • Target logistics unit in the inbound web service for goods movement
    Users will now be able to use web service for transferring one logistics unit to another logistics unit using web service.
  • Enhance BOM data extraction showing ECO effective start date & end date
    This will benefit the customer who are migrating BOM to other applications. Now they will be able to filter current and active BOM using the ECO effective date in the excel.
  • Adding Text and Text description of text Input products in BOM extraction
    Users will now see the 2 fields (text and text description) in the Input Product sheet in BOM extraction. Both are filled for text input product.
  • Adding Text and Text description of text Input products in BOM extraction
    Users will now see the 2 fields (text and text description) in the Input Product sheet in BOM extraction. Both are filled for text input product.
For detailed information about What’s New in the Supply Chain Management area, please click here.

Human Capital
Management


  • Enhanced Leave request with attachment feature
    Attachments can now  be added to the leave request and the same attachment for the leave request is available for approver review.
For detailed information about What’s New in the Human Capital Management area, please click here.

Financial
Management

 

 

  • Error Message for Clearing Documents in Closed Fiscal Year
    If the Posting Date of the original clearing document falls under a closed fiscal year, you cannot Reset Clearing document. 
  • PSM Read-Release & Write-Release for the Fields in Trade Receivables
    Payable Register Business Object 
    With this release the field Open Item Amount in company currency is available for read-release and Payment Baseline Date field is available in write-release in PSM.
  • PSM Write-Release for Field in ERM_EXPENSE_ARRANGEMENT Business Object
    With this release the field Expense Reporter Group Code in the ERM_EXPENSE_ARRANGEMENT business object is available for write-released in PSM.
For detailed information about What’s New in the Financial Management area, please click here.

Legal and
Compliance

  • Australia
    Updated Payment Time Reporting Scheme 
    The payment time reporting scheme has been updated and is now complaint with latest amendment act.
  • Austria
    Introduced New Version of optional file template JAB 4.0
    The new JAB 4.0 will enable companies in Austria to create the year end reporting data in a structured XML data.
  • Dominica, Malaysia, Greenland
    New Tax Number Types
    Customer will be able to add this newly created Tax Number Type For Customer and Supplier Accounts.
  • Germany
    Inclusion of Northern Ireland invoice In German OSS Vat return
    With this release the Northern Ireland invoice will be included in German OSS VAT, return. 
  • Mexico
    A29 Report changes to User Interface
    Customer will be able to use the hyperlinks to get detailed overview of documents related to it.
  • India
    E-way bill address update
    This change will enable that Address 1 and Address 2 maintained in the master data will be printed in the E-way Bill.
  • Singapore, Morocco, Vietnam, and Thailand
    ISO Standard Region Code Updates
    Updated region codes for Singapore, Morocco, Vietnam, and Thailand in alignment with the latest ISO notifications for country subdivision naming standards.
  • United States 
    ISO2022 Payment Format Update of Payment type Matching to ACH
    With this release the following payment types are updated from the ACH file format to the ISO2022 payment format CCR= "NURG", DAC= "NURG", IWI= "URGP", MTS= "URGP.
For detailed information about all country/region-specific features, please click here.

Solution Extensions

 

Take a look at the recently added partner solutions for SAP Business ByDesign in the SAP Store.

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model What’s New as of 2026-02-03
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community, 

As we move into February, it’s time to explore the latest updates and enhancements delivered during January 2026. In this edition of  What's New, I’ll walk you through the latest features and improvements delivered in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM).  

To stay up to date with previous blog posts from my colleagues, you can always go through and read them all using PaPMCloudUMWhatsNew tag. 

Let’s take a closer look at what’s new in February. 

Report  

Drag-and-Drop Reordering of Inserted Tabs in Reports 

You can now reorder inserted tabs within the Report page editor. 

This enhancement allows you to reorder inserted tabs using drag-and-drop directly within the Report screen. It improves flexibility and usability by enabling tab order adjustments without removing and reinserting tabs. This works directly within the Report screen once the tabs have been added. 

To reorder tabs, switch the Report Page to Edit mode. After inserting tabs, simply drag and drop them within the page editor to arrange them in the desired order. 

Home Screen Applications References in Reports 

You can now link Home Screen apps to Report Elements. 

A new button is available under Set Reference functionality in Reports, providing a drop-down list of Universal Model home page applications. This allows you to select and link an app in the same way as already existing references. 

This enhancement provides more flexibility to customize reports and navigation by directly connecting report elements with the relevant application from the home page application 

To create a link, switch the Report Page to Edit mode, hover over the grid, and select the Set Reference icon. 

In the Set Reference configuration window, open the Other tab. Choose Apps, and then select the required Home Screen app from the drop-down list. 

Data Screen 

Export numeric values to Excel without currency symbols 

You can now export report data to Excel with numeric values only, without currency symbols. 

In addition to the existing right-click export, a new export option Export to Excel – Numeric Values Only is available. When selected, amounts containing currency information in the report are exported as pure numeric values, allowing Excel to recognize them as numbers for easy validation, calculation and further processing without manual cleanup. 

The existing export functionality remains unchanged and continues to export values together with currency information. 

This is also applicable to export from Show Elements in Reports.  

Consistent column formatting in drill-down views 

As you already know, you can explore the detailed records behind aggregated key figures directly in the Data screen - in Pivot mode key figures are displayed as clickable links. When you select a key figure, a second list view (drill-down detailed view) appears at the bottom of the screen, showing the detailed data records that contribute to the selected value.  

From now on, column formatting defined in pivot tables is applied to drill-down detailed views in the Data screen.  

When you drill down to detailed records, the system inherits the column formatting settings from the originating pivot view. This includes display options such as “Text only”, “Value only”, “Text Last” and “Text First”. 

As a result, detailed records are displayed consistently with the aggregated pivot view, improving readability, and ensuring a uniform data presentation across different levels of analysis. 

To change the formatting for Grouped Row Data, hover over the Group field to see the menu where you can choose the required Text Arrangement option. 

To change the formatting for the Column labels, proceed as follows: 

  1. Choose the Column pane in the sidebar element and choose Edit (pencil icon) next to the field name.
  2. In the popup window that appears select required Text Arrangement from drop-down list with options.
  3. Choose Save or Cancel to discard changes. 
  4. Choose Refresh to apply the changes. 

After changing the formatting, the drill-down view must be re-applied by selecting the number for which you want to display details. 

This is also applicable to drill-down views in Show Elements in Reports. 

Dynamic hide and read-only conditions at cell level across Data Screen views 

You can now configure dynamic conditions in Adapt UI to control the visibility and editability of fields at cell level, with consistent behavior across both the Object Page and the Data screen view. 

Conditions defined in Adapt UI allow you to hide or show specific field values and to switch between editable and read-only states. The same logic is applied consistently to: 

  • fields on the Object Page, and  
  • corresponding cells in the Data screen. 

 When the defined conditions are not met, fields remain visible and editable in both views. All hide and read-only rules are saved as part of the Adapt UI configuration and persist after reload or re-login. 

You can base hide and read-only conditions on values of other fields within the same row, and the behavior updates after refresh when those driver values change. 

You can also remove or disable the configured conditions to restore the default state, where fields are fully visible and editable. 

Once defined for one record, the rules/conditions are applied across the entire dataset. 

Hidden Conditions  

Read-Only Conditions  

What’s New on SAP Help Portal 

We’re excited to announce that SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud now features a dedicated What’s New section on the SAP Help Portal. This new page complements the existing What’s New blog posts in the SAP Community and provides a centralized, structured overview of all product updates. 
The What’s New page offers a clear and comprehensive view of the latest features, enhancements, and content updates across the Standard Model, Universal Model, and Sample Content. Updated in line with the product delivery schedule, it gives customers a simple and reliable way to stay informed about ongoing improvements in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud. 

Proceed as follows: 

  1. Open the What’s New to SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud  page on the SAP Help Portal. 
  2. Under Release Type, select Universal Model to display only updates related to the Universal Model application.
  3. (Optional) Use the Scope filter to further refine the results: Application – updates relevant for all users; Sample Content – updates related to sample content; Administration – updates relevant for administrators.
  4.  (Optional) Under Type, filter the updates by New, Changed, Deleted, or Deprecated.
  5. (Optional) Use the Available as Of filter to select a specific date range and explore updates from a specific time period. 

Using these filters helps you quickly identify the Universal Model changes that are most relevant to your work and stay informed efficiently. 

 That’s it for this update! 

We hope these new features and improvements help you get even more value from SAP PaPM Cloud UM. Don’t miss our upcoming What’s New editions—we share fresh updates every month. 

Feel free to share this blog post with your colleagues and peers. Thank you for reading!

entry.title: Leveraging SAP Architecture Center for SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Good architecture doesn’t slow you down.  It removes friction.

That’s the idea behind Leveraging SAP Architecture Center for SAP Business Data Cloud—a sharp, partner-focused webinar kicking off our new data architecture miniseries.

SAP Architecture Center has just launched, with SAP Business Data Cloud front and center.  Inside, you’ll find solution reference architectures, diagrams, scenarios, service lists, and real examples—built to support how partners actually work.

In one session, we’ll:

  • Introducing the data architecture miniseries
  • Demo the SAP Architecture Center
  • Break down top SAP BDC use cases (data & analytics transformation, BW modernization, Databricks, AI)
  • Show how to reuse architectures in tools like draw.io
  • Explain how partners can contribute and shape what comes next

No fluff.  Just clear guidance to help you design better solutions, faster—and with confidence.

If you work with SAP BDC, this is worth your time.  Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jul/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010833.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPArchitecture #SAPBusinessDataCloud #SAPPartners #DataDriven

entry.title: Re: How Employee Absence date recalculation will work in...
entry.content:
Hi @ArdenT, There is 2 ways to recalc absences. For this specific Employee, there may be a tab in their People Profile Administer Time called Time Alerts ( you may need Permissions to see it). If there is a Time Alert is may have the Recalculate button there. That's the simplest way, if it works.

The more System Admin way is (there is both a Bulk way if you have many employees that need updating) or for an individual, here are the details: under MANAGE DATA and with the Employees number/identifier ID (we use User ID).

*Get before and after screenshots of the person's Leave you are recalculating, that way you can review it after the recalc. 😉

In MANAGE DATA, Create New: Time Management Recalculation Event.
 - External Code and Target ID are both "User ID" (or your systems unique employee identifier) 
 - Event Type: Recalc Absences
 - Target Type: User
 - Target ID: "Unique ID"
 - Date: I think it the beginning of the date you want the recalc to run from, you mentioned 2024.
 - Status: To be Processed
 - Schedule Job ID: think that's auto, leave blank.
 - Start Recalc on Save: YES
SAVE
And crosscheck screenshots and newly updated Profile Time Management screens.

Good Luck!

Not sure How are you selecting your dimensions. But if you select only the dimension and not the model  then the data checkbox is only about Master Data. Transaction Data can only be transported if you select Model with Data option.

But Its better to load master data in respective tenant unless its too much of an ask.

entry.title: Re: How to control automatic GI posting with conditions? EWM
entry.content:
Hello,

another option would be the GI report "/SCWM/R_ODO_POST_GI".

Instead of fiddeling around with PPF this might be an easier option - there you can select by warehouse number, specific document types or for your requirement for specific ship-to parties.

Be aware of package size (set to 1) and "Created by" date (date when the ODO was created, so depending on your szenario choose current date minus -5 or -10 days)

Setup as background job and let it run every 2 minutes or so.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/sewm/4190684926.html?locale=en-US 

Kind regards,

Chris

entry.title: From XSUAA to AMS: The Practical Path to Stronger Authorization
entry.content:
(Jana Subramanian serves as APJ Principal Cybersecurity Advisor and Compliance and has been recognized as a Fellow of Information Privacy (FIP) by the International Association of Privacy Professionals (IAPP). As part of his responsibilities, Jana helps with strategic customer engagements related to topics such as cybersecurity, data privacy, secure data flow, multi-cloud security integration architecture, contractual assurance, audit, and compliance.)

Introduction

SAP BTP security architecture is often explained in fragments: authentication here, authorization there, principal propagation somewhere else. But in real enterprise landscapes, these are tightly connected flows—and the exact sequencing matters. A small misunderstanding in where tokens are issued, how identities are propagated, or where decisions are enforced can quickly turn into inconsistent access, brittle integrations, and gaps in traceability, especially in hybrid (cloud + on-premises) environments.

In this blog, I’ll provide a comprehensive technical walkthrough of two complementary authorization models on SAP BTP, using numbered, end-to-end flows directly mapped to architecture diagrams:

  1. XSUAA-Based Flow — authentication, token routing, and principal propagation to on-premises systems
  2. AMS-Based Flow — centralized, policy-based authorization with CAP CDS enforcement

From there, we’ll zoom out to the “why”: where XSUAA fits best, why XSUAA alone can become limiting at enterprise scale, and what Authorization Management Service (AMS) adds—especially when you need consistent policy enforcement across applications, clearer separation of duties, and stronger auditability.

For the sake of clarity, this post intentionally stays at a high architectural level: I focus on concepts, sequencing, and decision points, and do not go into step-by-step configuration. The goal is to build a clear mental model you can reuse—before diving into implementation details in your own landscape.

XSUAA-Based Flow: End-to-End Identity and Principal Propagation to SAP S/4HANA (On-Prem)

This architectural pattern shows how a real end-user identity securely accesses an SAP BTP application and is then propagated to SAP S/4HANA on-premises—without falling back to shared technical users. That end-to-end identity continuity is a key foundation for zero-trust design principles and for auditable user accountability, especially in hybrid (cloud + on-prem) landscapes.

The goal is simple: the user who clicks in the browser is the same user recorded in SAP S/4HANA logs and authorization checks.

Note for clarity: This section focuses on the architecture and sequencing at a high level. Detailed configuration steps are intentionally out of scope to keep the mental model clear and reusable.

Systems involved

This XSUAA-based flow spans multiple components across cloud and on-premise boundaries:

  • Corporate Identity Provider (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID, Okta) – enterprise authentication source (password, MFA, conditional access)
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) – federation proxy between corporate IdP and SAP BTP trust
  • XSUAA (Authorization and Trust Management) – OAuth2/OIDC token service for SAP BTP apps (JWT issuance, scopes, role collections)
  • AppRouter + Application Runtime – application entry point, session handling, and token forwarding
  • Destination & Connectivity – secure routing and token exchange for outbound calls
  • SAP Cloud Connector – controlled, outbound tunnel into the on-premise network
  • SAP S/4HANA (ABAP) – backend authorization enforcement using ABAP roles and authorization objects

Figure 1: Authentication, token routing, and principal propagation via XSUAA

 

Architecture diagram: XSUAA flow

 Authentication steps (blue phase)

Step 1 — User access

The user opens the SAP BTP application in a browser. AppRouter acts as the entry point and checks whether a valid session already exists.

Step 2 — AppRouter triggers authentication via XSUAA

If no session is present, AppRouter redirects the user to XSUAA (/oauth/authorize). XSUAA then redirects to IAS as the trusted identity provider—following the OAuth2 authorization code flow:

  • AppRouter → XSUAA (/oauth/authorize)
  • XSUAA → IAS (trusted IdP)

Step 3 — IAS federates to the corporate IdP

IAS forwards authentication to the corporate IdP. This ensures:

  • password/MFA and corporate policies stay with the enterprise IdP
  • no user credentials enter SAP BTP

Step 4 — Authentication assertion returned

After validation, the corporate IdP returns a signed assertion/token to IAS containing the authenticated identity and relevant claims.

Step 5 — IAS issues an SAP-trusted identity token

IAS validates the corporate IdP response and returns a SAML assertion (or OIDC response, depending on setup) that XSUAA trusts for user authentication. XSUAA then issues the JWT access token.

Step 6 — Authorization code exchange and JWT issuance

XSUAA exchanges the authorization code for a JWT access token:

  • includes user identity, scopes, and role collections
  • is forwarded to AppRouter and the application runtime
    At this point, authentication is complete and authorization context is represented in the token.

Token and routing steps (green phase)

Step 7 — Destination selected

When the app needs to call SAP S/4HANA, it resolves the target destination (host, authentication type, proxy settings) via the Destination service.

Step 8 — Destination token exchange for backend compatibility

Based on destination configuration, SAP BTP performs the required token exchange to produce a backend-compatible credential, such as:

  • OAuth2 SAML bearer / assertion-based flows
  • principal propagation material (cert-based)
  • short-lived, target-specific credentials (not reused across systems)
    Critically, the user context is preserved, enabling traceability end to end.

Step 9 — Connectivity routing boundary

The outbound request is routed through SAP Connectivity service, enforcing the key boundary:
on-premise systems are not opened for inbound access from the cloud.

Principal propagation steps (red phase)

Step 10 — Secure tunnel via SAP Cloud Connector

The request traverses into the on-premise network via SAP Cloud Connector, using mutually authenticated transport and explicitly configured trust. This ensures:

  • cloud identity is cryptographically verified
  • only permitted resources are reachable
  • connectivity remains controlled and outbound-initiated

Step 11 — Principal mapped in SAP S/4HANA

Inside SAP S/4HANA, the propagated identity is mapped to an ABAP user (for example via certificate mapping rules). The important outcome is:

  • no shared technical user is required
  • the user identity remains intact across the boundary

Step 12 — Authorization enforced in ABAP

SAP S/4HANA performs authorization using native ABAP mechanisms:

  • ABAP roles/profiles
  • authorization objects (e.g., S_TCODE, business objects, org-level checks)
  • application-level authorization checks

Outcome: SAP S/4HANA logs and authorization traces reflect the real end user, not a generic system account—supporting strong accountability and audit readiness.

Key security benefits

  • True SSO experience across cloud and on-premise
  • End-to-end audit trail mapped to real users
  • No shared credentials with broad access
  • Authorization enforced where data lives (ABAP stays authoritative for ABAP)
  • Clear trust boundary with controlled, outbound-only on-prem connectivity

Part 2: AMS-Based Authentication and Centralized Authorization

In Part 1, we walked through an end-to-end XSUAA-based pattern where a user signs in through the enterprise identity provider and the application receives a trusted access token for calling services and backends. Part 2 keeps the end-user SSO experience (Corporate IdP → IAS) consistent, while shifting authorization to centrally managed policies. Authorization decisions are driven by centrally managed policies, not scattered across application-specific logic.

With Authorization Management Service (AMS), policy definition and evaluation are externalized into a central service. Your CAP application then enforces those decisions consistently through CDS-based authorization (for example, using CAP annotations such as @requires and @restrict). This approach helps teams implement fine-grained and consistent authorization across services while improving governance and auditability because policies can be reviewed and managed in one place.

Note for clarity: This section stays intentionally high-level and architecture-focused. Detailed configuration steps are not included, so the flow is easier to understand and apply across different landscapes.

Systems involved

  1. This AMS-based flow spans the following components:
  2. Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Enterprise authentication source (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID, Okta)
  3. SAP Cloud Identity Services
  4. Identity Authentication (IAS) – federation and login orchestration
  5. Identity Directory – user/group/attribute context used for policy evaluation (often synchronized from upstream identity sources)
  6. Authorization Management Service (AMS) Central policy store and policy evaluation engine
  7. CAP Application (with AMS integration) Application runtime that enforces policy outcomes via CAP CDS-based authorization (e.g., entities/actions annotated with @requires / @restrict)
  8. Destination Service Target-specific token exchange and connectivity preparation for backend calls (when required)

Architecture diagram: AMS flow

Figure 2: AMS authentication and centralized policy-based authorization flow

Authentication steps (blue phase)

The authentication phase remains the same as the XSUAA-based pattern—preserving the user experience and existing corporate IdP integrations.

Step 1 — End-user access The user opens the SAP BTP application. The application entry point (AppRouter + CAP runtime) checks for an active session.

Step 2 — AppRouter redirects to IAS If no session is present, AppRouter initiates sign-in using OIDC and redirects the user to Identity Authentication (IAS).

Step 3 — IAS federates to the corporate IdP IAS forwards authentication to the corporate identity provider using SAML or OIDC federation. Enterprise authentication controls (such as MFA and conditional access policies) are enforced at the corporate IdP.

Step 4 — User authenticates The user successfully authenticates with enterprise credentials. The corporate IdP validates the authentication request.

Step 5 — Assertion/response returned to IAS The corporate IdP returns a signed SAML assertion or OIDC response to IAS confirming the authenticated identity.

Step 6 — The application receives a trusted access token After successful sign-in, the application receives a trusted access token (commonly a JWT) representing the user identity and session context. The token is forwarded to the CAP application runtime.

Centralized authorization using AMS (green phase)

This is where AMS differs from a “token-only” approach. Tokens establish who the user is; AMS policies determine what the user is allowed to do and see. Instead of embedding authorization checks throughout each application, CAP can delegate policy evaluation to AMS and enforce the outcome consistently at runtime.

Step 7 —The CAP AMS integration (plugin) resolves the user’s assigned AMS policies and injects roles/filters for the request; CAP then enforces authorization via CDS rules.

Step 8 — Identity context and attributes resolved AMS builds the authorization context needed for evaluation, typically based on:

  • user attributes (e.g., department, region, cost center—depending on what your landscape provides)
  • group memberships (from Identity Directory and/or upstream identity sources)
  • policy assignments (which policies apply to the user and the requested resource)

Step 9 — Policies evaluated AMS evaluates centrally managed policies and determines the authorization outcome, such as:

  • permit/deny decisions for actions and services
  • fine-grained constraints and conditions (for example, patterns that can support entity/field/instance-level controls where applicable)

Step 10 — Decision returned and enforced in CAP The authorization outcome is returned to the CAP runtime and enforced consistently by the application:

  • decisions may be cached where appropriate for performance
  • only authorized requests proceed to data access and downstream calls This preserves a clean separation: policy is centrally managed and evaluated; enforcement happens reliably at runtime.

Backend access (purple phase)

Authorization is decided and enforced at the application/API layer first. If the authorized request requires backend access, the application then obtains target-specific credentials.

Step 11 — Target-specific token exchange (Destination Service) If the CAP application must call a backend (e.g., SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Business Data Cloud, SuccessFactors, or internal BTP services), Destination Service performs the appropriate, target-specific token exchange based on the configured trust relationship—issuing short-lived, target-compatible credentials.

Backend systems continue to enforce their own native authorization controls. AMS primarily standardizes and centralizes authorization at the application/API layer to reduce drift across applications and improve governance.

Trust relationships in the AMS architecture

A typical trust chain includes:

  1. BTP subaccount with IAS tenant (trust configuration for authentication)
  2. IAS with  corporate IdP (federation via SAML/OIDC)
  3. Destination Service with target system (target-specific trust and token exchange)
  4. AMS with Identity Directory (groups/attributes and policy assignments used for evaluation)

Why AMS matters at enterprise scale

As landscapes grow, authorization logic often becomes inconsistent across apps: duplicated rules, different interpretations of “who can do what,” and uneven auditability. AMS addresses this by enabling:

  • Centralized policy management – less drift across multiple services and teams
  • Consistent enforcement – CAP applies policy outcomes uniformly via CDS-based authorization
  • Fine-grained control – more expressive policies beyond coarse-grained token scopes
  • Stronger governance and auditability – policies can be reviewed, versioned, and managed centrally

Part 3: When AMS Beats XSUAA-Only Authorization at Enterprise Scale

Choosing between “XSUAA-only” authorization and an AMS-driven approach is an important architectural decision in enterprise SAP BTP landscapes. The key is understanding what each service is optimized for:

  1. XSUAA primarily provides identity and token-based authorization context (roles/scopes represented in the token).
  2. AMS primarily provides policy-based authorization decisions: what a user is allowed to do, under which conditions, and why.

In practice, XSUAA is excellent for authentication and issuing trusted access tokens, while AMS is designed to scale authorization governance through centralized policy definition and evaluation—especially when requirements become dynamic, conditional, and organization-wide.

 

Note on technical accuracy: In CAP, you’ll still use authorization constructs such as @requires and @restrict. The difference is that CAP remains the enforcement point, while AMS centralizes the policy decision logic and governance, reducing duplication and drift across applications.

Problems AMS solves

1) Role explosion

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

XSUAA authorization is commonly modeled with role collections and scopes. At enterprise scale, access needs quickly diversify by region, business unit, data domain, sensitivity level, and job function. If you try to represent all combinations as static roles (for example, Sales_Manager_EMEA_Read vs. Sales_Manager_APAC_Write), the number of roles can grow rapidly—making access management harder to maintain, review, and keep consistent.

How AMS helps:

AMS supports policy-based authorization where access rules can be expressed in terms of attributes, groups, and conditions. Instead of multiplying static roles, you can capture intent once, for example:

“Sales managers can access sales data for their assigned region.” Access is then evaluated dynamically based on user attributes/group assignments and policy conditions—reducing role sprawl and improving governance.

2) Hard-coded or application-specific authorization

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

Even when CAP annotations are used, real-world authorization often becomes application-specific: additional checks in handlers, custom logic per service, and authorization rules scattered across multiple codebases. When access requirements change, updates frequently require code changes, retesting, and redeployment, which slows governance and increases the risk of inconsistent implementations.

How AMS helps:

AMS enables teams to externalize authorization decision logic into centrally managed policies. CAP can enforce those outcomes consistently, while policy changes can be introduced centrally—reducing the need for every application team to re-implement or duplicate the same authorization logic. This reduces coupling between policy evolution and application release cycles.

3) Inconsistent enforcement across services and channels

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

When each application implements authorization slightly differently, the same user can receive different access outcomes depending on which application or API is used. Over time, this creates gaps, exceptions, and “shadow rules” that are difficult to detect—and even harder to justify.

How AMS helps:

With AMS, authorization logic becomes centrally defined and can be applied consistently across CAP services and APIs that enforce the same centrally governed policy model. This improves uniformity of access decisions and reduces the risk of policy drift between applications.

4) Audit opacity: “Why does this user have access?”

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

When authorization is split across role assignments, token scopes, and application-specific checks, it becomes difficult to answer audit questions such as:

“Why does this user have access to this data?” The answer often requires correlating role collections, reviewing code paths, and interpreting differences across multiple services.

How AMS helps:

AMS makes authorization more reviewable and explainable because policies are expressed as centrally managed declarations. This improves audit readiness: reviewers can assess policy intent directly, track changes over time, and relate authorization outcomes back to policy logic—without needing to reverse-engineer authorization behavior from multiple application deployments.

Aspect

XSUAA

AMS

Primary purpose

Identity and trusted token issuance; token-based authorization context (roles/scopes)

Central policy-based authorization decisions and governance

Authorization model

Role collections / scopes (token-centric)

Policy-based (commonly enforced through CAP CDS authorization patterns)

Where rules live

Often split between role assignments + application-specific checks

Centralized policies, enforced consistently at runtime

Granularity

Typically coarse-grained (role/scope level)

More fine-grained and conditional access patterns (including instance/attribute-driven controls where applicable)

Change management

Changes often require adjusting roles and/or application logic

Many access changes (policy assignment/composition) can be handled centrally; changes to the underlying CDS authorization model still require normal application change control.

Consistency across apps

Can drift if each app implements checks differently

Reduces drift through centralized policy intent and evaluation

Audit readiness

Harder to explain “why” across roles + code

Easier to review policy intent and changes centrally

Part 4: How AMS Meets Compliance Requirements

SAP Authorization Management Service (AMS) is built for enterprise-scale authorization governance: central policy definition, consistent enforcement, and reviewable access intent. That combination maps well to common assurance and internal control expectations—especially where organizations must demonstrate who can access what, under which conditions, and how those rules are managed over time.

Separation of Duties (SoD)

Assurance context: In many organizations, SOC 1 and SOC 2 expectations (and internal control requirements) include demonstrating separation between individuals who can initiate, approve, and execute sensitive business transactions—particularly when those processes impact financial reporting or operational assurance.

How AMS helps: With centralized, policy-based authorization, SoD constraints can be expressed as explicit, reviewable rules (for example, “an approver must not approve their own request”) and enforced consistently at runtime—without relying on ad-hoc checks scattered across application code.

Principle of Least Privilege

Regulatory context: Privacy and security frameworks commonly require access to be limited to the minimum necessary. For example, GDPR Article 25 (“data protection by design and by default”) emphasizes default protections and limiting unnecessary accessibility.

How AMS helps: AMS supports least-privilege patterns through policy conditions such as:

  • Conditional access — allow only when business conditions are met (e.g., status, ownership, org scope)
  • Context-aware decisions — incorporate user attributes (e.g., department, region, cost center) to avoid broad entitlements
  • Time-bound access — model temporary access by policy condition (e.g., time windows) to reduce standing privilege

Result: access can be scoped precisely to job function and context, rather than granted broadly via static roles.

Auditability and transparency

Assurance context: In assurance engagements such as SOC 1 / SOC 2, organizations typically need controls to be documented, reviewable, and operationally verifiable—including the ability to justify why particular access is granted.

How AMS helps: Centralized policy-based authorization improves audit readiness because:

  • Policies are centralized — authorization intent is easier to review than logic distributed across multiple codebases
  • Policy lifecycle is governable — change management practices (review/approval, versioning, traceability) can be applied to policy updates
  • Reduced dependency on deployments — policy review can be performed independently of application release cycles, minimizing the need to “read code” to understand authorization intent

In short: it becomes much easier to answer, “Why does this user have access?” by pointing to centrally governed policy intent rather than piecing together multiple implementations.

Zero Trust alignment

Framework context: Zero Trust shifts away from implicit trust and toward continuous verification and explicit authorization decisions for each access request.

How AMS helps: AMS aligns well with core Zero Trust principles:

  • Authenticated — identity is established through corporate IdP federation (commonly via IAS in SAP BTP architectures)
  • Explicitly authorized — access is evaluated against centrally governed policy intent (no “implicit trust”)
  • Evaluated at runtime — authorization is applied at the time of access, not only at session start

This supports a practical Zero Trust posture where every request is evaluated against policy and context.

 

Framework / standard

Typical requirement

How AMS helps (capability)

SOC 1

Controls relevant to services affecting financial reporting (e.g., controlled access, segregation expectations)

Central policy intent, enforceable conditions, consistent authorization outcomes

SOC 2

Security and access control expectations (governed controls, reviewability)

Centralized policy governance and consistent enforcement

GDPR

Data protection by design/default; minimize unnecessary access

Least-privilege policies and contextual access scoping

NIS2

Risk management measures and access control expectations

Central governance of access rules and consistent enforcement

DORA

ICT risk management expectations; protection and prevention controls

Governable policy lifecycle and reviewable authorization intent

ISO/IEC 27001

Access control expectations; least privilege; reviewability

Attribute-/condition-based authorization patterns and centralized policy management

Conclusion

XSUAA remains a strong foundation on SAP BTP for establishing application trust and issuing tokens that carry identity and role/scope context in XSUAA-based architectures.

As landscapes grow—more apps, more teams, more data domains, and more audit scrutiny—authorization becomes the harder problem. Enterprise requirements increasingly demand consistent decisions, fine-grained controls, and a clear way to explain why access was granted.

This is where Authorization Management Service (AMS) strengthens the architecture by enabling:

  • Centralized, policy-based authorization that scales without role explosion
  • Improved governance support through consistent policy management and clearer traceability for audits (e.g., SOC/ISO) and regulatory expectations (GDPR, NIS2, DORA)
  • Clear separation of concerns: identity (who you are), authorization policy (what you can do under which conditions), and application enforcement
  • Consistent runtime enforcement in the application layer (e.g., CAP CDS @requires / @restrict) based on assigned policies and context

Future-proof security architecture for SAP BTP

  • Target state (new CAP apps): IAS for authentication + AMS for centralized authorization policy
  • Coexistence: XSUAA remains relevant for existing apps/scenarios; adoption can be incremental

In short: keep sign-in consistent, modernize authorization where it matters most, and scale governance without scaling complexity.

References:

SAP BTP Developer Guide: XSUAA to AMS migration

AMS vs XSUAA: The Future of Authentication and Authorization in SAP BTP

CAP Authorization Documentation

entry.title: Production supply to next PSA in Cloud ERP
entry.content:
In many production environments, semifinished products are used immediately as components for a subsequent production step. Instead of putting these materials away into the warehouse after production, they can be supplied directly to the next production line upon goods receipt.

With Warehouse Production Integration with Synchronous Goods Receipt (Scope Item 5HO), SAP Cloud ERP enables exactly this scenario:
Warehouse tasks are created automatically during goods receipt, ensuring a seamless and efficient material flow from production to the next production supply area (PSA).

Why Use This Scenario?

Implementing this process offers several key benefits:

  • Reduced effort for end users, as fewer manual system steps are required

  • Elimination of errors caused by manual warehouse task creation
  • Faster processing due to reduced physical movements
  • A lean and streamlined production supply process

Overall, this approach significantly improves efficiency and transparency in production logistics.

 

Relevant scope item: Warehouse Production Integration with Synchronous Goods Receipt (5HO)

 

Configuration Overview

To enable this scenario, the following configuration steps and master data settings are required:

  • Activation of synchronous goods movements in the production scheduling profile

  • Maintenance of target PSA, destination bin, and warehouse in the production version of the product
  • Setup of automatic warehouse task creation upon goods receipt using the warehouse process type determination indicator in the warehouse product
  • Configuration of SSCUI 104392 – Determine Warehouse Process Type
  • Maintenance of a control cycle for the target PSA, including the destination bin

All these elements work together to ensure that the warehouse task is created automatically and the material is routed correctly.

Configuration:

  • Activation of synchronous goods movements in production scheduling profile

     

    • Target PSA and Destination bin and warehouse maintained in the production version of the product
  • Setup of automatic warehouse task creation upon receipt using warehouse process type determination indicator in warehouse product and setup
    • SSCUI 104392 – Determine Warehouse Process Type
  • Control Cycle for target PSA with destination bin

 

Example Scenario:

1. Step: Create Production Order

  • Material: SG424
  • Production Order: 1077350
    • Make sure to select the correct Production Scheduling Profile. 
    • Goods Receipt Location must be WM-managed Storage Location

2. Step: Confirm Production Order

  • Click on "Goods Movement Overview"
  • As you can see, there are two entries for goods movement. Goods receipt posting for material SG424 with movement type 101 and goods issue posting for component RM124 with movement type 261. 
  • Scrolling to the right, you will find the corresponding information about EWM Storage Bin, Target PSA and EWM Target Bin.  
  • After posting the goods movements, in the production order you will find the material document.
  • After posting goods receipt, there is an automatic warehouse task created to next production supply area
  • As maintained in the production version, after posting goods receipt, the material will be on PR-AREA. The destination bin is maintained in the control cycle. 

In conclusion, this use case demonstrates how semifinished products can be seamlessly transferred from production directly to the next production supply area without being put away into the warehouse. By automatically creating warehouse tasks upon goods receipt, manual steps are eliminated and the risk of errors during warehouse task creation is significantly reduced.

The article showed how the interaction of the relevant configuration steps and master data—such as the production scheduling profile, production version, control cycle, and warehouse process type determination—is essential to enable this scenario successfully. When these settings are correctly maintained, the synchronous goods receipt process ensures a fast, reliable, and efficient material flow.

As a result, customers can achieve a leaner production supply process, reduce physical movements, save valuable time, and work more efficiently in real-life production environments.

Questions or feedback are always welcome and highly appreciated!

Thanks for reading this blog post. Stay tuned!

Follow the PSCC_Enablement  tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts.

entry.title: Re: Utilizing SAP BTP Integration Suite for Integration with SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2
entry.content:
Hi Everyone, 

We have actually implemented this in production, for a reporting integration, after our event based reporting integration regularly clogged and the architecture became more and more complex than it should. 

So here's our real life experience tips.

Pagination logic is clear nowadays: Fetch 999 records, order by an identifier, take the last record's identifier's value and filter by that value in the next call, repeat until there are no records left.

Tip 1: If you set a logic like, if the number of records is less than 999, do not call the api anymore, which makes sense. However, sometimes APIs can return 998 (or less) instead of 999 and stop your whole pagination in the middle. Therefore instead of counting the arrays, keep fetching until there're no records left. 

Tip 2: In this case displayId was used as the identifier however, ordering by id (UUID) might be safer and more generic since some entities like emails don't have a displayId. But our favorite identifier proved to be "updatedOn". It is supported in all APIs. 

I hope that cursor-based pagination will be supported in the near future and we can leave all these innovations behind 🙂

entry.title: Re: MIRO posting for Demurrage condition (ZA90) posting t...
entry.content:
Hi @sapkey ,
My friend SAP is capitalizing your demurrage charges into inventory because the condition type ZA90 is currently set up as a delivery cost condition linked to the purchase order. In MIRO, such conditions are treated as part of the material valuation, so the system distributes the amount into inventory and material price difference accounts instead of expensing it directly to the mapped Demurrage G/L.

The steps to Fix / Configure Please follow the steps below
Check Condition Type Settings (Transaction M/06), Ensure ZA90 is not flagged as a delivery cost condition. If it should always expense, configure it as a statistical condition or non-inventory impacting.

Account Key & OBYC Mapping:
Verify the account key assigned to ZA90 in the pricing procedure. Map it to the Demurrage G/L in OBYC (transaction for automatic postings).

Invoice Posting Options:
If demurrage is unplanned, post it separately in MIRO as an unplanned delivery cost with direct G/L assignment. Decide whether demurrage should be capitalized (if it’s part of material acquisition cost) or expensed (if it’s a penalty/operational cost). Most companies expense demurrage, so condition type setup should reflect that.

Capitalizing Demurrage: Inflates inventory value and distorts material cost.
Expensing Demurrage: Provides clearer visibility of operational inefficiencies but requires proper condition type setup.
Incorrect Setup: Leads to reconciliation issues between MM and FI, and misstatement of inventory valuation.

entry.title: Impact of Time-Dependent Service Cost Level on Progress Calculation in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Hi experts,

I have a question regarding progress analysis and earned value calculation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for Professional Services projects.

Scenario:

  • A project activity (work package) has planned hours distributed equally across multiple months (e.g., 10 hours per month for 12 months = 120 hours total)

  • The assigned resource has time-dependent service cost levels (e.g., Period 1-6: 100 USD/hour, Period 7-12: 120 USD/hour)

  • We use POC (Percentage of Completion) method for progress tracking

Question:
When the system calculates earned value and determines POC, which service cost level does it use as the basis for total planned costs?

Option 1: The system uses the service cost level that was valid at the initial project planning date, and the total planned cost remains fixed (e.g., 120 hours × 100 USD = 12,000 USD) throughout the project lifecycle.

Option 2: The system dynamically considers the time-dependent service cost level for each period, so the total planned cost reflects period-specific rates (e.g., 60 hours × 100 USD + 60 hours × 120 USD = 13,200 USD).

Impact on Progress:
This affects how POC is calculated. If actual costs in later periods use the higher rate (120 USD/hour), I need to understand whether the "denominator" (total planned cost basis) stays at the original planning value or adjusts based on the period-specific cost level.

Has anyone worked with time-dependent pricing in professional services projects? Does the earned value calculation use a fixed baseline from planning or does it recalculate based on period-specific cost rates?

Any guidance or experience sharing would be greatly appreciated!

Best regards

This configuration is irreversible. Once you activate multiple address handling for SD documents in your tenant, there is no going back. It changes how the system handles partner data globally, not just for one project.

entry.title: Stock Transfer Order Delivery completed indicator
entry.content:
Scenario/ Business requirement
We use Stock Transport Orders (STO) to transfer stock from one plant to another (intra/intercompany). Sometimes the supplying plant cannot issue the full quantity in the first outbound delivery. Example: The STO is for 1,000 EA, but the supplying plant issues 900 EA (100 short). When the receiving plant posts the GR for 900 EA, the Delivery Completed indicator (ELIKZ) on the STO item remains open, and users must manually close the item.

Goal
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, we want an automatic way to close such STO items—e.g., if the remaining quantity has not been delivered within 7 days, automatically set Delivery Completed (ELIKZ) on the STO item so it stops appearing in open order backlogs.

 

We read SAP Note 1093582 (“Delivery completed indicator (ELIKZ)”) but there is no solution for SAP PUBLIC CLOUD in the note. Is there any possibility to configure this in Public Cloud?

entry.title: Re: ABAP Proxy generation support with SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Hi Prasanthi,

The Constraint is only design-time, not runtime.Existing SAP PO–proxy-based interfaces can remain  fully functional at runtime until you switch to point to CPI runtime endpoint

Before switching the SM59 destination to point to Integration Suite, ensure all existing PO-based proxies are:

  • Activated

  • Transported to QA/Prod

  • Not expected to change structurally anymore

As a next step do the switch to the Integration Suite and further configuration of logical ports and destinations with CPI end points once you have the corresponding ICO migrated or an Iflow configured.

Hope this helps.
